coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Zubair M.S.H.
Rahman, Raja Saifu
Shumaila,
Islam, Shama
Zulfequar, M.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper we have reported a simple, one step, non toxic, cheaper and greener route method to synthesize water soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using red lentils as a precursor through hydrothermal method for the first time. The prepared N-CQDs under UV lamp of the wavelength 365 nm shows bright blue fluorescent and quantum yield calculated comes out to be 13.2%. The as prepared N-CQDs shows excitation dependent emission properties and were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photon spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and Spectroflurophotometry. These N-CQDs gives high response to Fe3+ ions and lead to fluorescence quenching. This quenching mechanism was used to develop a nano probe sensor for successful sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated is 0.10 μM in the dynamic range 2–20 μM with correlation coefficient R2 (0.993). The stability of the present sensor was tested under the effect of ionic concentration and pH which shows that it is stable in high salt conditions and there is no effect of pH to our sensor. This sensor can play an important role in regulating concentrations of iron in order to monitor health of the human body.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent
Fe3+ sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and surface study of CQDs
Selectivity for the detection of Fe3+
Sensitivity towards the detection of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing one step hydrothermal synthesis of blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) TEM micrograph of CQDs at scale bar 50 nm (b) Diameter distribution of CQDs (c) FTIR spectrum of the prepared CQDs.
(a)Full scan XPS spectra of CQDs (b) High resolution spectrum of C 1s (c) High resolution spectrum of N 1s (d) High resolution spectrum of O 1s.
(a) XRD spectrum of CQDs in the range 10-800, (b) EDS spectrum of the CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs (Inset shows images of CQDs under day light and on UV irradiation at 365 nm) (b) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 420 nm).
(a) Fluorescence Life time decay curve of CQDs at excitation wavelength 360 nm (b) Decay curve of CQDs on adding 1 mM Fe3+.
Fluorescence stability of CQDs at (a) pH values (b) Concentration of NaCl (c) Storage time.

(a) Change in relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at excitation wavelength 365 nm on addition of different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 to 1000 μM.
Schematic diagram depicting the mechanism involve in quenching of flurosence intensity of N-CQDs on addition to Fe3+ ions.
(a) Plots of values of F0/F −1 at emission peak 448 nm versus the concentration of Fe3+ at excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (b) Linear plot of F0/F −1 versus Fe3+ in the range 2–20 μM.
Details of Fluorescence life time measurement.
Comparison of different fluroscent nanoprobe sensors for detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi, 110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper we have reported a simple, one step, non toxic, cheaper and greener route method to synthesize water soluble nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using red lentils as a precursor through hydrothermal method for the first time. The prepared N-CQDs under UV lamp of the wavelength 365 nm shows bright blue fluorescent and quantum yield calculated comes out to be 13.2%. The as prepared N-CQDs shows excitation dependent emission properties and were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photon spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and Spectroflurophotometry. These N-CQDs gives high response to Fe3+ ions and lead to fluorescence quenching. This quenching mechanism was used to develop a nano probe sensor for successful sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated is 0.10 μM in the dynamic range 2–20 μM with correlation coefficient R2 (0.993). The stability of the present sensor was tested under the effect of ionic concentration and pH which shows that it is stable in high salt conditions and there is no effect of pH to our sensor. This sensor can play an important role in regulating concentrations of iron in order to monitor health of the human body.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of nano materials with sizes below 10 nm. In recent years enormous attention have been drawn towards carbon quantum dots due to its unique physiochemical properties such as low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, water solubility, flexibility to surface modification, resistance to photo bleaching and excitation wavelength dependent emission [1–6]. Due to these properties and wide range of application it is an alternative nano material which can replace the traditional dyes and toxic heavy metal based semiconductor quantum dots. CQDs are superior as compared to the traditional dyes in terms of aqueous solubility, surface modification and photo stability [7]. These properties make it a suitable nanomaterial which can be used in the wide range of applications such as in bio imaging, photo catalysis, drug delivery and in sensing applications [8–14]. After careful evaluation of existing literature it has been concluded that various techniques and type of the materials was used to synthesize CQDs. These material includes natural products, toxic chemicals and different technique for synthesis route includes chemical ablation [15,16], electrochemical carbonization [17,18], laser ablation [19,20], microwave irradiation [21,22] and hydrothermal treatment t [23,24]. Among these processes chemical ablation requires multiple steps and electrochemical carbonization requires use of the strong acid [25]. Microwave irradiation is limited because of uncontrolled reaction conditions and poor control over sizes. At present among the reported techniques the hydrothermal technique has came out to be less expensive, environmental friendly and easy route to synthesize the novel carbon quantum dots from various precursors without the requirement of any toxic chemical solvent [26]. Many organic compounds such as chitosan, glycerol, citric acid, polybasic acid have been utilized for the synthesis of CQD [3]. These organic compounds are limited due to post surface passivation, toxicity and strict reaction conditions. Keeping these factors in mind, methods and materials which are simple, environment friendly, inexpensive and eliminates rigid condition are highly desired. For this reason, green method using precursors directly from nature is a good idea for large scale synthesis and for wide range applications. Nature has abundance of carbon which makes it cheaper material to be used and gives researcher a freedom for new ideas and different ways to develop high quality stable quantum dots. A wide range of natural precursor such as garlic [27], orange juice [24] eggs [28], milk [4], apple juice [29] plant leafs [30] has been used for synthesis CQDs. The key challenge is to develop CQDs with high quantum yield and stable photoluminescence through greener route. Recently much focus has been given in synthesizing doped carbon dots and especially nitrogen doped CQDs. Nitrogen having five available valence electron can form bonds with the carbon atoms. Nitrogen doping can tune the reactivity, surface and electronic properties. This nitrogen source can be obtained by the mixing of aqueous ammonia and glucose for several days [33]. This method is however time taking and not eco friendly which restricts its use in the different applications. So there is a need to utilize nitrogen from natural sources. Therefore there is a space to search and utilize nitrogen from naturally occurring precursor which can serve for both carbon and nitrogen source in order to synthesize the luminescent carbon quantum dots for large scale synthesis. Some natural materials have different compounds in it and the CQDs made from them possess various surface groups which enhances the optical properties. Here we have reported a green synthetic approach towards the synthesis of carbon quantum dots using red lentils.

Red lentils are an edible pulse and are the earliest food mankind has been consuming. Lentils are rich in protein, fats and carbohydrates and hence are a good source of naturally occurring carbon and nitrogen which is abundant in nature.

Meanwhile, Toxicity of heavy metals has become a threat to environment and human body. Out of heavy metal ions iron is one of the problems to human health due to alarming increase of it in environmental water. Both excess and deficiency of iron in human body can lead to biological disorders [31]. Fe3+ metal ion may cause cell oxidation and annihilation of blood circulation in human body [32]. Thus to monitor human health detection of Fe3+ in biological and environmental samples is important. Currently various techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy and electrochemical method have been used for detection of Fe3+ [33,34]. However these techniques require complicated procedure for sample preparation and are expensive which limits their wider applications. Sensor based on fluorescence spectrometry quenching has been in great demand due to its fast response, low limit detection and simplicity. These fluorescence based Fe3+ sensors can be made by mixing fluorophore moiety with ionophore moiety. The ionophore moiety selectively binds Fe3+ to form Fe(III) fluorophore complex which in turns reduces the intensity of fluorophore [35]. Lv and co authors synthesized nitrogen doped fluorescent CQDs using ethanediamine and citric acid as precursors for carbon [32]. Waheed ullah khan and co authors synthesized green emitting CQD for Fe3+ detection with limit of detection (LOD) 19 μM in linear range 0–300 μM [8]. Yang and co authors made Fe3+ nano probe sensor from cellulose fibers with LOD 0.96 μM in linear range 25–250 μM [36]. In contribution towards detection of Fe3+ we have made fluorescence based Fe3+ sensor using carbon quantum dots from red lentils through hydrothermal method. This sensor is cheaper, non toxic and made from easily available red lentils thus making it suitable for large scale production. Further this sensor shows good sensitivity and selectivity and can even work on high ionic concentrations. As low as 0.10 μM concentration of iron can be detected from it in aqueous solutions.

The metals salts CdCl2 (98%), ZnCl2 (97%), CaCl2 (98%), NaCl (98%), CoCl2 (98%), and KCl (99%) were purchased from S D fine chem Limited India. HgCl2 (99.5%), MnCl2 (99%), LiCl (99%), and SnCl2 (98%), were purchased from Chemical drug house Ltd. India. CuCl2 (99%), FeCl3 (98%), NiCl2 (99%), Glacial acetic acid (99.5%), HCl (36%), NaOH (97%) and CH3COONa (98%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. De ionized water was used throughout the whole experiment.

Transmission Electron microscope (TEM) micrographs and Energy Dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were taken using JEOL JEM-F200 at accelerating voltage of 200 KV. The samples for TEM were prepared on carbon coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was taken from SPECS surface nano analysis GmbH(Germany) using 100 W Al -Kα radiations. Fourier Transform Infra red spectroscopy (FTIR) was observed using Thermo Scientific, NICOLET iS50. UV–Vis spectroscopy measurements were taken from CAMPSEC double beam scanner. All fluorescence measurements were carried out from Cary Eclipse spectrometer from Agilent Company.

Red lentils were purchased from local market of New Delhi India. Typically 2 g of red lentils were washed with de ionized water twice and then dried at room temperature. A fine powder of it was made using mortar pastel. This powder was mixed with 40 mL of de ionized water and put on stirring at room temperature for 30 min. After this the solution was transferred into a 60 mL Teflon lined autoclave in hydrothermal and was put in a furnace at temperature of 200 °C for a time period of 5 h. The hydrothermal was allowed to cool naturally. Finally we get a carbonaceous solution which was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to discard larger and undissolved particles and supernatant solution was collected. This supernatant solution was further passed through 0.22 μm syringe filter. The obtained solution was light brown in color and was diluted with ultrapure water for further use.

Quantum yield (QY) of any fluorescent material is calculated with reference to a compound of known QY. If the excitation wavelength of both the samples is kept same then QY can be calculated using equation (1) [37].
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y
R
η
2
η
R
2
I
A
A
R
I
R

Where QY is quantum yield of CQDs, η refractive index of solvent, I is integrated intensity and A is absorbance at excitation wavelength. The subscript R refers to parameters of reference compound. Here in this paper QY of CQDs was calculated using Quinone sulphate (0.1 M H2S04) which has known quantum yield 0.54 at excitation wavelength 360 nm.

The current experiment was performed at room temperature at pH 7 in sodium acetate buffer (Na-Ac) (0.2 M, pH 7). Typically 80 μL CQDs was mixed with 1 mL of Na-Ac buffer followed by addition of different concentrations of metals and further different concentrations of Fe3+. The final volume was raised up to 3 mL by adding de ionized water into it. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were noted after 3 min of incubation time. Further stability, selectivity and sensitivity of the prepared nano probe sensor were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface study of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , CQDs were prepared through one step hydrothermal treatment of red lentils at 200 °C for a time period of 4 h. The morphology and size distribution study was carried out through TEM.

Fig. 2 (a) shows typical TEM micrograph of the prepared CQDs which confirms the formation of small sized particles. The particles are found to be spherical in shape with average diameter in the range of 6 nm .Fig. 2 (b) shows size distribution of CQDs with maximum particles are of sizes in the range 4–10 nm.

Fourier Transform infra red spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was carried out in order to confirm the functional group attached to the surface of CQDs. Fig. 2(c) shows FTIR spectra with characteristic peaks at 3400 cm−1, 2952 cm−1, 1654 cm−1,1460 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1. The broad peak at 3400 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H [38,39], whereas peak at 2952 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of C-H bonds [40]. The absorption peak at 1654 cm−1 is assigned to C=O [41], peak at 1460 cm−1 is conjugated C=C and peak at 1035 cm−1 is assigned to C-O bond [42].

Further in order to confirm the various groups present on the surface of CQDs, X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to have information about the elemental composition, oxidation states and groups present on the surface of CQDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows full scan XPS spectra having three distinct strong peaks at 285.10 eV, 399.52 eV and 531.62 eV which is assigned to binding energies of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s respectively. Further high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s in Fig. 3(b) illustrate the presence of four peaks at 284.86eV, 285.70 eV, 286.75 eV and 288.42 eV which corresponds to bonds C-C, C=C, C-N/C-O and C=O respectively [43,44]. The N 1s high resolution spectra shown in Fig. 3(c) has three peaks at 399.52 eV, 400.11 eV, 401 eV which reveals the presence of C-N-C, N-C3 and N-H bond [45]. The O 1s spectra in Fig. 3(d) displays two peaks at 532.42 eV and 533.75 eV which is assigned to bonds C=O and C-OH/C-O-C [46]. These results confirm the presence of amide and hydroxyl group present on the surface of CQDs which may have arises due to carbohydrates and proteins present in red lentils. These results are in agreement with the results obtained from FTIR.

For structural analysis X-ray diffraction measurement was performed. The XRD spectrum exhibited a broad peak at 2Ɵ = 220 that is attributed to the 002 Bragg reflection as depicted in Fig. 3(a). The interlayer spacing along this plane is 0.32 nm which is comparable with graphite interplanar spacing. These results reveal the amorphous and graphitic nature of the prepared carbon quantum dots.

Further EDS was also performed in order to confirm the presence of elements present in the prepared carbon quantum dots. From Fig. 4 (b) it is clearly seen that along with carbon and oxygen there is a presence of nitrogen which supports the doping of nitrogen in CQDs as seen from XPS analysis. There is also some peaks corresponding to copper because the sample for EDS measurement was prepared on copper grid.

Optical properties of the prepared CQDs dispersed in water were studied and is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The graph between wavelength vs. absorbance shows a strong absorption peak at 340 nm due to π-π* transition of carbon core. Under normal visible light the color of CQD is light yellow but under illumination of UV lamp (365 nm) it shows a bright blue luminescence as shown in inset photograph. This blue luminescence is consistent with other prepared CQDs [3–5].

Fig. 5(b) shows fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs under different excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 420 nm. The emission spectra under the excitation of 360 nm show strong emission intensity with emission peak at 448 nm. Similar to other CQDs these CQDs shows excitation dependent emission characteristics. At excitation from 280 to 340 nm there is no change in emission peak though there is change in intensity. But at excitation from 340 to 420 nm, emission peaks shift towards longer wavelength with gradual decrease in intensity. This behavior is due to presence of different organic groups on the surface of CQD and also due to random distribution of its different sizes. The phenomenon responsible for blue fluorescent in carbon quantum dots is still in debate. From the literature reported earlier, the isolated sp2 clusters whose size is less than 10 nm can results in bandgap which is in accordance with blue fluorescence because of electron hole pair localization [47]. Apart from surface and size effects, nitrogen atom having electron withdrawing nature due to presence of five valence electron, plays an important role in blue fluorescence [48]. To further study fluorescent properties, the quantum yield (QY) of the N-CQDs was calculated. The QY is found to be 13.2% under excitation of 360 nm using reference compound Quinone sulfate (in 0.1 M H2S04, QY = 0.54).

Time dependent Fluorescence life time spectra were recorded to study the quenching behavior of CQDs. Fluorescence decay curve was measured at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The decay curve was best fitted with tri-exponential fit and is given by following expression
(2)
N
(
t
)
=
A
1
exp
(
t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
( t /
τ
2
)
+
A
3
exp
( t /
τ
3
)
Where τ1, τ2 and τ3 are different decay times; A1, A2 and A3 are corresponding amplitudes.

Fig. 6 (a) shows decay curve of CQDs and Fig. 6(b) shows decay curve of CQDs when Fe3+ was added in it. Parameters of fitting and calculated values are shown in Table 1 . The average life time (τ avg) of the prepared CQDs is calculated to be 3.51 ns . While after adding 1 mM Fe3+ in CQDs the average life time decreases to 3.24 ns. Low decay time may have come due to radiative depopulation because of band edge recombination [49] .It is observed that there was no significant change in life time after addition of Fe3+, which ignores excited state interaction and confirms static quenching mechanism with formation of complex between fluorophore and Fe3+ in ground state [50].

The average life time was calculated using the given equation
(3)
τ
a v g
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
+
A
3
τ
3
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
+
A
3
τ
3

Further stability of the prepared CQDs was studied under various conditions. Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity was noticed by adding different solutions of pH ranging from 3 to 13. Fig. 7 (a) shows that on addition of different pH the intensity is almost same and no significant interference of pH with CQDs is found, hence present sensing system is found to be insensitive to pH and therefore we have chosen pH 7 for our sensing system. Here F0 is peak intensity of CQD and F is peak due to addition of external reagents which is different pH in this case.

For ionic stability different concentration of NaCl ranging from 0 to 1 M was mixed with CQDs and fluorescence spectra was recorded. From Fig. 7(b) this is clearly visible that under high ionic concentration there is no change in intensity of CQDs. This confirms that prepared CQDs is stable under high ionic strength. Further the sample was incubated for one month and fluorescence spectra were noted at regular interval. Fig. 7(c) shows no remarkable change in intensity with incubation period. These all above discussed results shows that these CQDs can be used for different analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for the detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to make an effective sensor, selectivity is an important parameter to be studied. For this we have analyzed the change in fluorescence intensity of CQDs by adding different metal ions to it. Different metal ion solution was prepared by adding corresponding metal salt to water and stirred it for 5 min. The concentration of prepared metal salt stock solution was 1 mM we have made stock solution of metal ion such as Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe3+,K+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Zn2+,Mn2+,Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+. All the prepared metal ions with concentration 1 mM were added to 100 μL of CQDs and 500 μL buffer solution and the volume was raised to 2.5 mL by adding water. Fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. From the spectra it was observed that there was no significant change in intensity of CQDs on adding various metal ions except Fe3+. On addition of Fe3+ the fluorescence intensity gets quenched. Fig. 8 (a) shows histogram of relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) on addition with different metal ions where F0 is fluorescence intensity of CQDs and F is intensity due to addition of different metal ions. From Fig. 8(a) it is clearly seen that except Fe3+ all ions have negligible effect in emission intensity.

The possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching due to addition of Fe3+ ion is due to the fact that Fe3+ ion binds to the hydroxyl group present on the surface of N-CQDs which results into formation of Fe(III) complex chelate. After that there is a transfer of electron from the excited state of N-CQDs to half filled 3d orbital's of Fe3+ resulting into non radiative electron hole pair recombination and this process results into fluorescence quenching [32]. Fig. 9 shows schematic diagram depicting mechanism of fluorescence quenching. From the photographs of cuvette in Fig. 8 it is clearly seen with naked eye that on addition of Fe3+ in CQD solution, intensity reduces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity towards the detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the sensitivity of the prepared fluorescent CQDs, the effect on intensity of CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ was analyzed. Different concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 1000 μM at pH 7 was added to CQDs solution and corresponding fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelength of 360 nm were taken. From Fig. 8(b) it is clearly seen that as concentration of Fe3+ increases the emission at 448 nm gradually decreases, this confirms that the sensor is sensitive to Fe3+. Fig. 10 (a) relation of F0/F −1 with Fe3+ concentration shows good linear response in range 2–20 μM and 300–1000 μM. Here Fo and F is emission intensity peak at 448 nm in absence and presence of Fe3+. The whole range here did not fit linearly indicating that both static and dynamic quenching process take place in the present sensor. Correlation coefficient (R2) is found to be 0.993 in the range 2–20 μM and quenching efficiency was defined by the following equation (4).
(4) F0/F −1 = 0.00377(Fe3+)+ 0.06859

To calculate the lowest limit of detection (LOD) we have applied 3 times σ/K ratio i.e LOD = 3σ/K [32] where σ is standard deviation and K is slope of linear range 2–20 μM in Fig. 10 (b). The LOD was calculated and comes out to be 0.10 μM and this reveals that present sensor has good lowest detecting limit of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Table 2 compares the results obtained from different sensors and sensor reported in this literature. Overall the results obtained in this paper are highly selective and sensitive to Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium. According to U.S environmental protection agency the highest amount of Fe permissible in drinking water is 5.4 μM [35]. In our present sensor we can detect as low as 0.10 μM successfully. This indicates this sensor can play important role in regulating the concentrations of iron in order to monitor health of a human body.

Herein; we have prepared nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from truly a greener method by hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the first time without any chemical modification. This greener route method is cost effective, non toxic and environmental friendly which can be used for large scale production. The prepared N-CQDs show excitation dependent behavior in the range 280–420 nm and is also blue fluorescent under UV light with a quantum yield of 13.2%. In addition, this N-CQDs works as nano probe sensor for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with as low as 0.10 μM concentration of Fe3+ can be detected in the linear range 2–20 μM. Therefore this method has come out to be effective tool for the synthesis of biocompatible CQDs for Fe3+ sensing.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Dr. Uday Deshpande for XPS measurements, ESCA and Molecular Spectroscopy Lab, UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Indore, India. One of the author R.S.R is thankful to IUAC New Delhi, India for providing TEM facility.
